coredata.dc:title: Facile preparation of low temperature carbon dots with long-wavelength emission and their sensing applications for crystal violet
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Tianze
Dong, Yuanyuan
Su, Yewenqing
Li, Yang
Wang, Jiaming
Hu, Jing
Li, Jialin
coredata.dc:description: Crystal violet (CV) is one of the main components of common fungicides in daily life, which has inhibitory effect on gram-positive bacteria. However, CV remains in the environment for a long time and have potential risk of disease. Therefore, it is necessary to develop effective methods for detecting CV. Low-temperature carbon dots (LT-CDs) are studied to provide a new idea for the development of CDs green preparation technology from the perspective of low energy consumption. In this experiment, LT-CDs with long-wavelength emission were prepared based on the oxidation, cross-linking polymerization and Schiff base reaction using o-phenylenediamine and hydroquinone as carbon source at low temperature, and were characterized by various techniques. It was found that LT-CDs could be used as a fluorescent probe for quantitative detection of CV based on the inner filter effect, and the practicability of the method was verified by real samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Low-temperature carbon dots
Long-wavelength emission
Crystal violet
Fluorescent probe

Structure and composition of the LT-CDs
Possible quenching mechanism for detecting CV

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by Heilongjiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (LH2023E113), Doctoral Fund of Heilongjiang Institute of Technology (2021BJ02 and 2021BJ03), and the Student Innovation and Entrepreneurship Training Program of Heilongjiang Province (202311802A122). The authors would like to thank Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the support of materials characterizations. The authors also thank their colleagues and other students who participated in this study.

The authors would like to thank Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the support of materials characterizations. The authors also thank their colleagues and other students who participated in this study.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM micrographs of LT-CDs; (B) FT-IR spectra of LT-CDs; (C) XPS spectrum of LT-CDs; XPS high-resolution spectra of (D) C 1 s, (E) N 1 s, and (F) O 1 s of LT-CDs.
(A) UV–Vis spectra of LT-CDs; (B) PL spectra under different excitation wavelengths of LT-CDs; Inset: the normalized PL intensity of LT-CDs under different excitation wavelengths.
PL spectra of LT-CDs solution at different ionic strengths (from 0 to 5 mol/L). Photostability of the LT-CDs under continuous excitation at 410 nm for 60 min.

(A and B) PL spectra of LT-CDs with different concentrations of CV; (C) The relationship between (F0-F)/F0 and CV concentration; (D) linear correlation plot in the CV concentration ranging from 0.02 to 12 μmol/L.

Selective detection towards CV using LT-CDs (CV, the analogues of CV and metal cations are 20 nmol/L); The full name corresponding to the abbreviation: malachite green (MG), leucocrystal violet (LCV), leucomalachite green (LMG). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) The PL spectra of LT-CDs, and absorption spectra of CV; (B) The PL decay profiles of LT-CDs before and after addition of CV; (C) The PL response of LT-CDs toward CV at different temperatures; (D) The PL spectra of LT-CDs before and after addition of CV.
Schematic representation of the preparation and the sensing applications of the LT-CDs.
An overview on recently reported based nanoprobe for determination of CV.
Results for the determination of CV in real samples.

head.ce:title: Facile preparation of low temperature carbon dots with long-wavelength emission and their sensing applications for crystal violet

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials and Chemical Engineering, Heilongjiang Institute of Technology, Harbin 150050, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Materials and Chemical Engineering, Heilongjiang Institute of Technology, Harbin 150050, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of the preparation and the sensing applications of the LT-CDs.

Crystal violet (CV) is one of the main components of common fungicides in daily life, which has inhibitory effect on gram-positive bacteria. However, CV remains in the environment for a long time and have potential risk of disease. Therefore, it is necessary to develop effective methods for detecting CV. Low-temperature carbon dots (LT-CDs) are studied to provide a new idea for the development of CDs green preparation technology from the perspective of low energy consumption. In this experiment, LT-CDs with long-wavelength emission were prepared based on the oxidation, cross-linking polymerization and Schiff base reaction using o-phenylenediamine and hydroquinone as carbon source at low temperature, and were characterized by various techniques. It was found that LT-CDs could be used as a fluorescent probe for quantitative detection of CV based on the inner filter effect, and the practicability of the method was verified by real samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The primary aim of investigating the hazards and treatment of pollutants is to screen and identify contaminants. Crystal violet (CV) is an essential pharmaceutical ingredient that is one of the main components of everyday fungicides [1,2]. CV has been used successfully to eliminate parasites and fungus in aquatic goods in numerous nations and locations since the 1930 s [3]. Furthermore, the dyeing characteristics of CV have been extensively researched for applications in the textile, paper, and pharmaceutical sectors [4]. CV and its metabolites, on the other hand, persist in the fish body and environment for a long time and pose carcinogenic and mutagenic concerns [5]. Currently, numerous nations and regions have imposed prohibitions or limits on the use of CV in aquaculture. As a result, in order to protect humans and aquatic creatures, an efficient method for detecting CV residues in wastewater must be developed.

Some approaches for determining CV, such as immunosorbent assay, microextraction combined with high performance liquid chromatography, and liquid chromatography coupled with electrospray ionization tandem mass spectrometry, have been proven in recent years [6–8]. However, these procedures are hampered by sophisticated apparatus, time-consuming pretreatment operations, and/or long detection times. The advantages of photoluminescence (PL) techniques are their simplicity and speed. For example, Han et al. reported that t the preparation of yellow-emitting PL silicon nanoparticles [9]. These nanoparticles exhibited significant quenching when exposed to CV due to the synergistic effect of inner filter effect (IFE) and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). Based on this observation, a new method for determining CV was established.

In recent years, as a new member of the family of PL nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted important attention owing to several advantages such as outstanding optical properties, water dispersibility, excellent biocompatibility, and so on [10–15]. Based on these advantages, CDs have been widely used for sensing, bioimaging, drug delivery photocatalysis, and other fields [10,14,16–18]. The concept of Green Chemistry has gained immense popularity in recent times. It emphasizes on the development of new materials that comply with the principles of low-carbon footprint and environmental protection [19]. This requires reducing the energy consumption during the preparation process and minimizing the use of toxic and harmful substances. Therefore, the traditional method of preparing CDs, which involves high energy consumption, is not suitable for future development. Instead, the strategy of preparing CDs with low-temperature CDs (LT-CDs) is being adopted. The study of LT-CDs is aimed at providing a new way for green preparation of CDs with the goal of low energy consumption. LT-CDs refers to CDs whose preparation temperature is lower than 100 °C. On the one hand, the temperature is much lower than the typical carbonization points of organic compounds. Therefore, CDs structure is formed independent of high temperature carbonization process, which is convenient for large-scale preparation. On the other hand, the temperature is lower than the boiling point of water, which increases the safety of the experiment. However, many LT-CDs preparation processes now require oxidizing agents. In the later purification, it is difficult to remove the excess oxidant completely, which leads to the great harm to the environment. Therefore, it is an urgent need to develop a real LT-CDs method that satisfies the concept of Green Chemistry.

Long-wavelength emissive CDs (λem > 500 nm) offer significant benefits over conventional emission (λem＜500 nm) CDs in applications such as biological imaging and optical sensing [20]. They provide better imaging quality by improving penetration depth and reducing radiation damage. As a result, developing efficient long-wavelength emissive CDs is highly desirable for further advancements in these fields. CDs are prepared using o-phenylenediamine (o-PD) as the carbon and nitrogen source. The phenyl group of o-PD has a conjugated structure that creates a sp2 domain, which enhances the band emission of CDs [10]. The amino group also provides hydrophilic and N-doped functional groups, which help to improve the optical performance of CDs. Furthermore, the combination of o-PD and other aromatic ring compounds can produce CDs with even better performance[10]. O-PD is a high-quality carbon source that is widely used in the preparation of CDs at high temperature [21]. It is also used in detecting environmental pollutants. Zuo et al. have used a F-doping strategy to modify the optical properties of CDs produced from o-PD and 1,2-diamino-4,5-difluorobenzene, which served as the carbon source at 180 °C [22]. The study found that F-doping affects the redshift of CDs for PL emission and that CDs could selectively combine with Ag+. As a result, CDs have become highly sensitive probes for detecting Ag+ in aqueous solutions and various biological systems. In addition, researchers are continuously exploring the preparation of LT-CDs derived from o-PD.

Herein, based on the polymerization of o-PD and hydroquinone (HQ) at low temperature, LT-CDs were prepared without the involvement of other special instruments and oxidants, and the as-prepared CDs show the long-wavelength emission (λem = 556 nm) under excitation at 410 nm. The yields of LT-CDs are about 18 wt%, and their quantum yields are 24 %. Remarkably, CV could efficiently quench the PL of LT-CDs with excellent selectivity. Based on these, a sensing platform for detecting CV is developed using LT-CDs as nanoprobe. LT-CDs show practicability for the highly selective and sensitive detection CV in real samples with satisfactory results. The preparation and the sensing applications of the LT-CDs are presented in Scheme 1 .

Typically, o-PD (1.0 g) and HQ (1.0 g) are added into 60 mL water to form a transparent homogeneous solution under ultrasonication. After reacting at 60 °C for 3 h, a brown suspension is obtained. The obtained solution is centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 5 min to remove the large molecules. The sample is freeze-dried to obtain powder. Further, the crude product is purified using silica column chromatography. A mixture of methylene chloride and methanol (20:1) is used as the eluent. The gradient elution technique is employed by gradually increasing the polarity of the solvent. To prevent cross-contamination between different CDs, a slow flow rate of 2–3 s per drop is maintained throughout the separation process. After removing the solvent via rotary evaporation and drying under vacuum at 40 °C, we obtained CD products with a yield of 18 wt%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PL assay of CV with LT-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To perform the CV assay using LT-CDs as a PL probe, follow this procedure. Mix various concentrations of CV solutions with 3.0 mL of LT-CDs solution (10 μg/mL) in a PB solution with a pH of 7.0. The total volume of the mixture should be 4.0 mL. Next, record the PL spectra by exciting at 410 nm. Investigate the system's selectivity by adding other species under the same conditions.

The experiment involved collecting water samples from the environment. The collected samples were processed by centrifugation and filtration through a 0.45 μm filter membrane. Subsequently, three solutions of CV with different concentrations were prepared using the filtered water as a solvent. Each solution was mixed with LT-CDs solution in a 1:3 ratio and transferred to a quartz cuvette for testing. The testing was done under 410 nm excitation to record the fluorescence intensity. Finally, the amount of CV recovered and the percentage of recovery were calculated using the linear equation. Prior to use, the carp fish tissues underwent pretreatment. The fish muscles were minced and frozen, and 5 g of the thawed muscles were placed in a beaker with 15 mL of acetonitrile and sonicated for 60 min. The resulting supernatant was collected and centrifuged, then mixed with anhydrous sodium sulfate before evaporating the acetonitrile. The remaining sample was dispersed in PB. For CV analysis, the prepared sample was combined with the standard CV solution and the CD dispersion. Other detection procedures were carried out as previously described.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure and composition of the LT-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: LT-CDs were prepared in this experiment using o-PD and HQ as precursors, without requiring any special equipment or additional oxidants. When exposed to air, HQ undergoes oxidation and gets converted into p-benzoquinone [23]. On the other hand, the amino group of o-PD is highly reactive and can easily form various oxidation products through oxidation or polymerization reactions in the air [24]. During the reaction, a Schiff base is formed between the –NH2 group of the o-PD oxidation product and the CO group of p-benzoquinone. The heat generated by the Schiff base reaction promotes polymerization and oxidation reactions. The intermediate formed during the reaction continues to react with the o-PD oxidation product and p-benzoquinone to eventually form LT-CDs.

During the production of CDs, a small number of precursor molecules can over-polymerize and form larger particles [25]. Therefore, it is essential to perform centrifugal operation before further purification. Silica gel chromatography is a recent technique for CD purification that separates molecules based on their physical and chemical properties, rather than their size. In this study, we purified the CDs using centrifugation and silica gel column chromatography to achieve greater purity.

In order to examine the structure of CD products, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) is utilized. TEM is an effective method to determine the shape, morphology, and size of CDs [26]. The TEM micrographs, shown in Fig. 1 A, reveal that the CDs are nearly spherical and well-dispersed. Specifically, the LT-CDs are spherical nanoparticles with an average size of 3.5 nm. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) measurements were conducted to determine the surface states and compositions of the LT-CDs. The FT-IR spectra in Fig. 1B show that the stretching vibration of O–H/N–H occurs at 3500–3300 cm−1, the stretching vibration of CN occurs at 1637 cm−1, the stretching vibration of CN/CC occurs at 1528 cm−1, the stretching vibrations of C-N occurs at 1364 cm−1, and the stretching vibration of C-O occurs at 1134 cm−1. These absorption bands indicate the formation of polyaromatic structures and the presence of many nitrogen-containing groups [19]. FT-IR spectra have confirmed the presence of a Schiff base structure in LT-CDs, which is evidenced by the CN band. This confirms that the formation of LT-CDs involves a Schiff base reaction [23]. Furthermore, the element composition of the LT-CDs has been analyzed using an X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectrum, which shows that there are peaks of C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s located at approximately 285 eV, 400 eV, and 531 eV, respectively (as shown in Fig. 1C). The high-resolution XPS spectra shows that the C 1 s band (Fig. 1D) can be divided into four different binding energies of CC/CC at 284.2 eV, CN at 285.1 eV, CO at 286.0 eV, CN/CO at 288.5 eV [27,28]. The presence of CN confirms that the Schiff base reaction has occurred in the formation of LT-CDs. The N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 1E) shows four peaks at 398.3, 399.2, 400.0, and 401.1 eV, attributed to pyridinic N, amino N, pyrrolic N, and Graphitic N, respectively [29,30]. The O 1 s band (Fig. 1F) consists of two peaks at 531.6 and 533.0 eV for CO and CO, respectively [30–32]. The presence of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups confirms that LT-CDs have good water solubility.

Fig. 2 depicts the optical properties of LT-CDs. In the UV region, LT-CDs reveal absorption peaks at 261 nm (Fig. 2A) that arise from the π–π* transitions of CC bonds in their aromatic ring structure [19,33,34]. Additionally, LT-CDs exhibit a distinct absorption peak at 445 nm. The adsorption bands in the low-energy region of 360–700 nm arise from the n–π* transition of the conjugated CN bonds, and these absorption bands are typical for CDs [35]. Fig. 2B displays the PL emission spectra of CDs excited by various wavelength. Inset of Fig. 2B is the normalized PL intensity of LT-CDs under different excitation wavelengths, and demonstrates the relation between the excitation wavelength and the normalized PL intensity. That is, the PL intensity increases as the excitation wavelength changes from 380 to 450 nm, reaching a maximum value before decreasing again. However, it is worth noting that unlike many other previously reported CDs [36], the emission peaks of LT-CDs do not shift when different excitation wavelengths are applied. These results are often attributed to the emission from surface states or defects [37,38]. LT-CDs present pH-dependent PL behaviors (Fig. S1), which further confirms the role of surface state in the PL emission [19,39].

The stability of CDs plays an important role in their practical applications [40]. Under extreme conditions, the PL feature of CDs can easily change. However, according to Fig. 3 (red region), the normalized PL intensity of LT-CDs remains higher than 0.9 at different ionic strengths. Additionally, the normalized PL intensity of LT-CDs remains extremely stable even after continuous irradiation by ultraviolet light (Fig. 3, blue region). These results proved that the prepared CDs have good storage stability, photostability, which make LT-CDs suitable for a wider range of real applications.

CV can be used to efficiently quench the PL of LT-CDs. It provides a possible for us to establish a PL nanoprobe for CV via turn-off mode. Then, we carefully researched PL responsiveness of the LT-CDs for CV in 0 to 50 μmol/L range. In Fig. 4A and 4B, the relationship between the PL intensity of LT-CDs and the concentration of CV is presented from the two-dimensional. Both Fig. 4A and 4B indicate that the PL intensity of LT-CDs at around 556 nm is gradually decreased with the addition of CV, indicating that the nanoprobe is sensitive to CV concentration. The relationship between the PL quenching ratio (F0-F)/F0 and CV concentration is addressed in Fig. 4C and D, and a fine linear correlation occurs over an appropriate concentration range of CV (0.02–12 μmol/L), providing a linear regression: (F0 − F)/F0 = 0.0427C + 0.0097 with R2 of 0.994 (Fig. 4 D). The detection limit is calculated to be 7 nmol/L (see supplemental materials for details) based on the ratio of signal-to-noise of 3, which could compare gratifying with those of previous reports for CV detection in the Table 1 .

In Fig. 5 , the ability of LT-CDs to selectively sense CV is demonstrated. The sensing system does not respond significantly to the analogues of CV and metal cations do not impede the CV sensing process. This indicates that LT-CDs have a favorable selectivity towards CV.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible quenching mechanism for detecting CV
ce:section.ce:para: In order to understand how detection works, thorough investigations are conducted. As observed in Fig. 6 A, the absorption band of CV in the range of 450–650 nm has a significant overlap with the emission spectrum of LT-CDs. This overlap suggests the possible occurrence of FRET or IFE [44]. The FRET process typically leads to a decrease in the fluorescence lifetime of the donor, whereas the lifetime remains unchanged in the IFE process [45]. In the presence of CV, the fluorescence decay profile of LT-CDs remains unchanged indicating that the fluorescence lifetime is constant. Hence, IFE is the main quenching mechanism rather than FRET as shown in Fig. 6B. Fig. 6C shows that the PL response of LT-CDs to CV is temperature-independent, supporting IFE [46]. Furthermore, Fig. 6D shows that upon adding enough CV, the shape of the PL spectrum changes. This change occurs because CV, as a PL absorber, has maximum absorption at 591 nm and insufficient absorption at 650–750 nm. As a result, a new PL peak of the LT-CDs/CV mixture occurs at around 630 nm. This phenomenon can also be attributed to IFE [4].

The practicality of the sensing system was evaluated by examining the spiking recoveries of Hulan River and fish tissues samples. Spike-and-recovery method was used in this study by spiking with a known amount of CV solutions (0, 1.0, 5.0 and 10.0 μmol/L) into real samples before the pre-treatment step and the results were exhibited in Table 2 . LT-CDs-based sensing procedure showed high accuracy and credibility with recoveries ranging from 97.0 % to 104.1 %. and the relative standard deviations (RSDs) are below 3.1 %, These results showed that the proposed LT-CDs had significant potential practical applications.

To summarize, LT-CDs were prepared by polymerizing o-PD and HQ at lower temperatures, eliminating the need for special equipment or oxidizing agents. These LT-CDs exhibit exceptional selectivity, with CV effectively quenching their PL. A sensing platform for detecting CV was developed using these LT-CDs as a nanoprobe based on IFE. To make the sensor practical for use, the assay of CV in environmental water and fish samples produced good results. This study offers a practical approach to creating long-wavelength emissive CDs and expanding their applications in toxicant sensing.

Tianze Li: Data curation, Funding acquisition, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Yuanyuan Dong: Conceptualization, Data curation. Yewenqing Su: Formal analysis. Yang Li: Formal analysis. Jiaming Wang: Formal analysis. Jing Hu: Software. Jialin Li: Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Heilongjiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (LH2023E113), Doctoral Fund of Heilongjiang Institute of Technology (2021BJ02 and 2021BJ03), and the Student Innovation and Entrepreneurship Training Program of Heilongjiang Province (202311802A122). The authors would like to thank Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the support of materials characterizations. The authors also thank their colleagues and other students who participated in this study.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2024.123863.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Portable smartphone platform based on Ti3C2 MQDs/CDs assembly for ratiometric fluorescence quantitative monitoring of crystal violet

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Yellow emissive Se, N-codoped carbon dots toward sensitive fluorescence assay of crystal violet
